, writing for the Los Angeles Star, December 13, 1856, said:
In the prosecution of collecting furs along this coast by
a large party of traders and hunters composed of Russians, half-breeds and Codiac Indians
, were in 1818, hunting and trading at the
jealousies sprang up and with them difficulties and broils occurred between
were simple and harmless, their numbers insufficient to cause internal discord, and their remote situation offered no opportunity to cultivate the science of war.
were the reverse of harmlessness and simplicity and without difficulty or loss on their part exterminated all of
which they could discover except a small number of females whom they carried away with them on their departure.
"Two men and two women were undiscovered in their hiding place and after the sailing of
Their numbers were increased by the slow but gradual course of nature until 1836, when
consisted of seven persons, four male and three females,
of about twenty years of age was of a fairer tint than
who from 1830 to 1836 in the prosecution of their business along the shore of
sometimes extended the scene of their operations to
the timid half-breed maiden of San Nicolas
occasioned considerable sensation among their fellow hunters who had not visited
, or who, on their visits had not been so fortunate as to discover
who invariably sought to avoid the observation of strangers.
Repeated attempts were made by hunters at various times to induce
to hold intercourse with them but which were only partially successful.
, set out for the purpose of bringing away
. After much delay and difficulty in accomplishing this object they succeeded in getting six of the seven persons on board
, a part Scot, part English sea captain then living in
where from the change of diet and habits they soon sickened and died, with the exception of

became blind in consequence of his sickness but instinctively found his way to the ocean's shore, where, recovering his health, he for many years was the most notable object that met the eye of the visitor or traveler at
. He was unfortunately drowned by falling from a steep bank into the ocean.
lived for many years in the family of
one of the most respectable citizens
in whose care she was at the time of her death.
in consequence of her flight from those who brought away her relatives, remained there in regal solitude.
She was frequently seen by the hunters who visited
in subsequent years, but all efforts to hold communication with here [her] were unsuccessful, as she invariably fled from the approach of every human being.
, who for many years had been engaged in otter hunting and had frequently visited
, found her, old, infirm and decrepit, suffering from hunger and succeeded in removing her from
, which appeared in the Star (n.d.) and was reprinted in the Evening Bulletin,
in 1811. It was at this time the difficulties arose between

in that year, it may well be that
was mistaken in the name of the captain. Three other ships, the
on board, were in the Island waters taking otter during 1810-1811. One account states that the firm of
. Frankly I do not know who was responsible for the alleged slaying of most of
. The fact remains that by 1825-1836 there was only a handful of

, stated in his article, "The Lone Woman of San Nicolas Island," December 20, 1892, that the schooner,
and had been chartered for the trip by
. He also said the captain was
also accompanied them, and they set sail with the blessing of
, about August 1, 1835. (The latter statement on the part of
, is, I believe, somewhat erroneous since, at the time when the
at this time, but he was a hired field overseer, not the spiritual head of
in January 1833, did not take any post at
until the spring of 1842, seven years after
arrived off the north shore of
, presumably about the 3rd or 4th of August, 1835, and
were glad to be taken off. There is no harbor worthy of the name on

. The waters about the place are treacherous. Ground swells are heavy, and at times the surf pounds the sweeping crescents of sandy beaches on both sides with tremendous force and high waves, making landing in small boats almost impossible. There is one very tiny enclosed harbor known today as
being about midway on the north shore) where small row boats can put in, or at least could before the tiny refuge sanded in. Entrance to
was through a narrow passage in the rocks, and it was just about wide enough for a small skiff. Here in times past
, no doubt, guided their plank canoes to a safe landing. Here too was a small spring, welling out of the ledges—good, sweet water; the last potable spring before
It may well be that the
offshore while the ship's boat went into

. The water beyond the breakers was too deep to afford good anchorage, and in certain seasons of the year, squalls rise suddenly, making it imperative for larger craft to stand out to sea to avoid being swept sahore [ashore]. Stories vary as to how the "
" came to be left on shore.
One version has it that when
were assembled at the landing place,
, about twenty-five years of age, suddenly broke away from the group and started back west along the north shore.
asleep in one of the huts, and in the excitement of embarking preparations, had forgotten
The other story is that all of
, uttering a scream of anguish, plunged overboard and swam back to shore to get

fearing the stormy warnings of a dropping glass, stood out to sea and cruised up and down as long as he dared. The storm increased, and the huge breakers pounding the beach precluded any attempts to launch the ship's boat. So the
to take refuge in the lee of
, where she disembarked the refugees. Manana they would return for
as in all Latin controlled countries, manana, is truly the tomorrow that never comes. Days merged into weeks; weeks became months; months turned into years; shipping became scarce along
was sunk. Gradually the memory of
became a sort of a folk tale, which remained alive only in the minds of the inhabitants of the sleepy little villages of
. Yes, someday someone would go out and rescue her. But as the years drifted by, no one ever did.
By the time of the gold rush the story of how this
, a passenger on board the steamship
made this entry in her journal for Thursday, May 3, 1849:
there catching otter; a storm coming up they were obliged to put out to sea, leaving
on shore. They afterwards returned and got them all off but
, who has lived there alone ever since.
Some years ago they caught her but she was perfectly wild and had lost her speech, so they left her
No doubt other stories in circulation at the time were just as vague and off beat as this one. Luckily we do have more correct versions of the final chapter in the life drama of
this last inhabitant of San Nicolas
who were in the search party that found
have left us their stories of what happened.
, alias Charles Brown, a native of
, were the men who eventually tracked the
to her isolated hut of whalebone and brush standing on the bleak, windswept ridge comprising the west end of
During the remainder of his lifetime
gave a number of interviews in which he related the particulars of his finding of the "
, by which name she has been known to history ever since.
Perhaps the best account ever published concerning the discovery and bringing back to civilization of
. It appeared under the title "Eighteen Years Alone in
, September 1880. It was reprinted in pamphlet form in 1950 by
. There are discrepancies in her account, as indeed there are in all such yarns.
added to or left out details in his various versions and I shall not attempt any lengthy comparison of these accounts in this brief resume of the incident.
. In May 1830 he was one of a group of trappers that left

. He continued to hunt, however, and had many adventures on the water and ashore. He went to the diggings in the spring of 1848, but the deaths of so many companions by fever drove him out. In 1849 he again returned to try his luck, but his luck didn't hold out. He bought
in January 1850. In this he returned to
, where he had decided to settle. In the year 1851
went on several short otter hunts. Shortly after purchasing
he bought out the interests of a man by the name of
, who had a sheep ranch on
with sheep, cattle, hogs and horses. Thisranch [This ranch] he maintained for seventeen years. In those days the titles to the islands of

were clouded, to say the least. Men bought and sold the rights to occupy them without having the least vestige of a title to the lands. They built ranch houses and made other improvements in spite of the fact that
, were all the possessions of the
. However, such little matters as lack of title did not deter these pioneers from claiming
, and a crew of two Indians. At that time sea gull eggs were in great demand, and since there were many gulls on
they anticipated a good harvest. It was on this trip that
saw the first evidences that the
was still alive. Let him tell the story in his own words, as he dictated it to
September 1, 1878, and published and annotated by
in The Life and Adventures of George Nidever,
and having arrived early in the day,
, one of the Indians, and I landed and travelled along the beach towards the upper end of

some 6 or 7 miles. As a short distance from the beach, about 200 yards, we discovered the foot prints of a human being, probably that of a woman as they were quite small. They had evidently been made during the previous rainy seasons as they were well defined and sunk quite deep into the soil then soft, but now dry and hard

also found the weather-beaten remains of three small circular brush enclosures about six feet in diameter, with walls standing about five feet, grass growing inside them and giving no evidences of having been occupied for some time. Apparently these were the temporary shelters built by
, which she used from time to time when absent from her more permanent resident on the west end of
. On poles ranging from four to eight feet in length, which were thrust into the ground around these jacales, were pieces of sun dried seal blubber which

estimated had been there about three or four months. These windbreaks were located about two miles apart, and stood on slight elevations back from the beach, perhaps two or three hundred yards. On this expedition
states he landed on the south side of
may have been lying off the stretch of beach we know as
, which is a harbor in name only, and is probably the only semi-decent anchorage on that side of

. (We camped at this place November-December 1940, excavating a small, open, badly eroded village site on a slight elevation back a few hundred feet from the beach. From this place we took quantities of sea-grass cordage, coarse matting, fragments of short sea-grass frontal skirts and basketry. It may well have been one of the places where
had one of her temporary camps, but l believe the material we excavated from the shell heap antedated her lonely vigil.)
When the schooner's anchor began dragging,
improvised one by filling a large sack with stones. The gale which sprang up blew for eight days, at the end of which they left their uncertain position and ran over to
. The egg hunting had not panned out so well on
as they had anticipated, and they had no better luck on the rocky
, so they headed for home, minus their eggs but satisfied the "
The news of the discovery of the evidence of
, who by this time was in charge of
decided to outfit for another trip to
the following winter (1852), this time to take sea otter which apparently had staged a comeback in the waters around
to make every effort to find
, or as then called, Charley Brown.
Again they landed on the south side of
, and headed toward the west end. At the head of

forged ahead. He rounded the end of the long sandy ridge, which terminates in a very rocky point at the extreme west end, and started down the north side, headed east. Here on the sandy beach he saw fresh footprints, but he lost them in the heavy carpeting of ground cover. He also found a small piece of drift wood which
had evidently lost from her bundle as she made her way inland. It was on this trip that
had first sighted some seven or eight wild dogs, about the size of a coyote, but they were black and white in color.
thought that perhaps the animals had killed and eaten

, since they had seen no further signs of her. But discovery of the footprints, as well as the finding of a basket tucked away in the crotch of a high malva bush, convinced them she was indeed still alive. The basket was covered with a piece of seal-skin, and in the container were several shag-skins, neatly trimmed into squares, along with some bone needles and a long, tightly twisted sinew rope.

started to replace the basket in the bush, then changed his mind, and scattered the contents upon the ground. He left the basket beside them—explaining that if on their next visit they came to this place and found the items replaced, they would know she was alive. They then returned to

. Next day they searched again, but did not find her. Then they began hunting otter, which were very thick. After hunting three or four days, a strong southeaster came up, and continued for about a week. Rough water made hunting impossible, so once more they left
. This time they ran over to
, where they rook refuge in a good harbor (presumably
) and laid by until the gale blew itself out. Afterwards they returned to
It was not until the summer of 1853
made his third attempt to find the
. This time he was successful. With him went
because of his florid complexion; and
This time they landed about mid-way of
, on the north side, or approximately where
is located. They were equipped to remain for two or three months, and wanted not only to find the
, but to take sea otter as well.
The first day they made tentative explorations along the shore toward the west end and, when within about one-half mile of the extreme western trip of
, they found fresh footprints in the damp soil around a spring of water. This spring is, or was known in the 1930's, as
, to distinguish it from the largest spring on
, which is a mile or so east of
are never constant. Over the years they have changed according to the fancy or the lack of knowledge of the namer.
, but the fresh signs were encouraging. Early next morning all hands, except the
, went ashore and headed west toward the head of
striking across to the spot where the basket had been found in the malva bush on the previous trip.
) kept on toward the western tip of land, skirting the north shore. Later
reported they found the basket with its contents carefully replaced in the bush.
is a tiring business. I know, because I have trudged every foot of beach on the place, as well as having crossed and recrossed the plateau on top of
got tired. He sat down to rest while

In those days the surface of the terrain was not as barren as it now is. Low brush and grass covered the western hills, which are at present windswept, sandy ridges, where the sun reflects glaringly on bright days and over which the sea gulls mew in lonely flight against a backdrop of sullen gray clouds during squally weather.
plodded on until he reached the very tip of

, then he turned south and began climbing the long slope. The ridge is broken by a succession of two or three rounded terraces. En route to the summit he passed the brush-covered, whale-rib framework of several jacales, long since abandoned, with grass growing high within the open, deserted huts. (Brown's statements concerning tall grass growing on the dunes at that end of
seem almost incredible today because those areas are now bare and as devoid of covering as a Chihuahua dog.)
looked about him in all directions. Ahead of him, on what was approximately the highest point of the ridge,
saw only . . . "a small black object about the size of a crow, which seemed to be in motion." Said
: "What is that? It looks like a crow sitting on a whale bone." He continued toward the object:
So when I came to find out it was her I took my gun and stuck my hat on and held it up to make signs to
I did not know if she would bite or scratch.
to come along. She had a brush fence about two feet high to break the wind and right in front she sat facing me. The sun was coming in her face. She was skinning a seal before I came up to her."
Looking about in all directions from this point he (
) discovered at a distance, along the ridge, a small black object about the size of a crow which appeared to be in motion. Advancing cautiously towards it, he soon discovered it to be

, her head and shoulders only, visible above one of the small enclosures resembling those we had before discovered. He approached as near as he dared and then, raising his hat on his ramrod, signalled to
who were then recrossing the low sandy stretch and were plainly visible from this point.
They saw the signals and came toward him. In the meantime,

was busily employed in striping the blubber from a piece of seal skin which she held across one knee, using in the operation a rude knife made from a piece of iron hoop stuck into a piece of rough wood for a handle. She kept up a continual jabbering to herself and every few moments would stop and look in the direction of
, whom she evidently had been watching, her hand placed over her eyes to shade them from the sun.
Upon his first approach there were some dogs near which began to growl. These
sent away with a yell but without looking in the direction of
having come up, they quietly surrounded her to prevent any escape
The long search was at last ended.
approached. Instead she bobbed her head and smiled ingratiatingly, chattering away in her unintelligible island tongue. Not one of the
to climb the slope, but he was tired. He couldn't see what they had found and didn't much care. His feet were tired, and whatever it was could wait until they returned. But the signals from
became more insistent. Reluctantly he made his way to the top of the hill. When he arrived,

bowed and smiled at him. Apparently she was quite happy and, remembering her duties as an Indian hostess, she busied herself around the fire which was burning in the center of the enclosure. In the embers she placed some roots of two different kinds . . ."one called carcomites and the name of the other I do not know . . . As soon as they were roasted she invited us all to eat some."
The site of her hut was ideal for keeping watch over the west end of
"The inclosure or hut where we found her was on the N.W. side and near the top of the ridge that forms the upper end of
. It was not far from the best springs of water, near to the best point for fish and seal, and it commanded a good view of the greatest portion of
At this point I am going to break in upon Nidever's narrative and relate my own experiences in the re-discovery of the hut site once occupied by
, I set out on horseback to ride to the west end of
with another of our fellow Westerners, Ex-Sheriff
as leader of the biological division, had landed on
on July 22, and since I was anxious to get busy on the archeological reconnaissance of
, I gladly accepted Lamberth's offer of a horse. With his generous services as guide, we started out from our camp near the
, headed west along the beach, covering practically the same route taken by
In my knapsack I had a copy of Ellison's The Life and Adventures of George Nidever, published in 1937. It proved a valuable guide to the landmarks we visited. We passed
, near which is a spring of sweet water, thence to
, from which a pipe line about two and a half miles long conducted drinking water to the
. This entire area is covered with the remains of ancient camp-sites, where the
used all of the sources of water mentioned. At last we came to the end of
In my journal I noted: "The point of
is one long sand dune. Here in this ridge was the site of Juana Maria's hut. I photographed the site (ridge) from the east. We ate lunch at the
where the spring issues from a shelving rock just at the edge of tide line. This is
. It is a sandy beach in summer but in winter is covered with drift wood. Abalone by the thousands cluster along this section of shore line on the rocky reefs.
"Today it dawned sunny, but the fog rolled in, and the sun came through only in intervals. In winter the gales lash the northwest end and heavy seas pound the beaches, tossing the spray inland."
We passed remnants of old whale-bone hut structures, which I duly noted. As we rode up the hill I remarked to

was correct, then we should find the remains of Juana María's house on that high point." When we reached the spot I had indicated, there on the windswept summit were some nineteen pieces of whale-bone, pieces of ribs and scapulae which were scattered on the ground in a rough circle. Since it was the only evidence of any shelter on the site which corresponded to Nidever's description, it seemed logical that there could be only one answer, this was the wreckage of the whale-bone and brush shelter once occupied by the
In other respects too, the site occupied by the hut corresponded to the description of
who found it in 1853. Southwest of it, within plain sight is an enormous sea lion rookery and the incessant gutreral barking of the animals came to us between blasts of wind. From that rookery

obtained some of her sustenance. There too were the places where the sea gulls and shag nested, and west and southeast of us were the rocky reefs teeming with shell-fish and other sea life. Also plainly visible was the stretch of beach where
was located, and further east along the coast were the other springs mentioned by

had kept her supplies of dried meat tucked into crevices in the rocks and staked out on driftwood poles. From all of these sources of water she carried the life-giving fluid back to her campsites in the pitched grass water bottles which she wove endlessly.
Later, on a second trip to
December 1, 1940, we again visited this place, and photographed the jacal site on top of the ridge. Then we re-erected the fallen pieces of whale bone, and a member of the party,

who was short in stature and possessed of a shock of wiry black hair, sat down in the center of the enclosure and we photographed her. In the picture all that can be seen of the girl was her black hair appearing over the top of the whale bone, just as described by

. In 1949, I again visited the ridge, and found that the heavy whale-bones had again been blown flat by the blasts of the northeast gales. There we left them to gradually weather away and become part of the debris carried by wind and sand over the west end of
But to revert once more to
to go with them. She was not unwilling. Said
(he was then forty-one years of age.

, in case she would not come could not we take her by force. I said no, she is not afraid. So I took her by the shoulder and said Vamoose and she understood at once. I took everything she had and she took a big seal head in a basket and that was all. We all had something to carry. Then she had a little brand of fire and she took that away and wobbled along with a strange kind of step."
adds to the picture with more detailed observations:

"Just outside the inclosure or windbreaks, as I should call it, was a large pile of ashes and another of bones, showing that this had been her abode for a long time. Nearby were several stakes with blubber on them, as we had seen around the others (inclosures). There was blubber also hanging on a sinew rope, similar to the one already described (The one found in the basket.

) which was stretched between two stakes. Near the inclosure were several baskets, some in the process of construction also two bottle-shaped vessels for holding water; these as well as the baskets being woven, were of some species of grass very common on
. (The grass in question was the sea grass already mentioned.

). There were also several other articles such as fishhooks made of bone and needles of the same material, lines or cords of sinews for fishing and the larger rope of sinews she no doubt used for snaring seals on the rocks where they came to sleep . . .

was of medium hieght, but rather thick. She must have been about 50 yrs. old, but she was still strong and active. Her face was pleasing as she was continually smiling. Her teeth were entire but worn to the fums [gums], the effect, no doubt, of eating the dried seal blubber. (

was only partially correct in the latter diagnosis. Much of the attrition of the teeth of the California Indians came from the pulverized stone dust from the mortars in which the tribesmen pounded their seeds and dried meat and shell fish which mingled with their foodstuffs.

"Her head, which had evidently been for years without any protection, was covered with thick matted hair, that was once black, no doubt, but now it had become a dull brown color. (Who was it that once remarked that except for a certain pigmentation of the hair all Chinamen would be red headed?

"Her clothing consisted of but a single garment of the skins of shags made in the form of a gown. It fitted close at the neck, had no sleeves, was girded at the waist with a sinew cord, and reached nearly to the feet. She had another dress of the same material and make in one of the baskets. These were sewed with sinews, the needles used being of bone. This place was undoubtedly where she usually lived, but in the rainy season she lived in
nearby." (Various references have been made to
in 1939-1940 never located it. I suspect it was either in the rough narrow canyons on the south side of
or possibly in the "Roughs" on the east end. I regret very much never having seen this shelter.
"She at once began putting her things into her baskets. Her basket filled she put in on her back and followed

toward the beach, while we walked behind; each of us carrying some of her things. Seal meat, some of it stinking, and a seal's head from which putrefied brains were running, was all carefully put into the basket. (And, my friends, nothing stinks worse than a rotting seal carcass.

). We soon arrived at a spring of water where we stopped and on some stakes which we found standing near we hung the things we were carrying, fixing them on the stakes in such a manner as to lead her to believe we took very great care of them. (This was
, the nearest source of water to Juana Maria's hut site and the last potable water along the northwest side of
issues from a shelving rock, just below the bank." This was the largest spring on
. It comes out of the rock formation at the base of a steep bank. Here
and I remained on the bank above. This being finished, we proceeded to the boat and went on board
, she crept forward to the bow where she knelt, holding firmly on to either side of
. As soon as we got on board, she crept along side of the stove which was on deck."
went about their job of hunting sea otter.
was perfectly content among her new friends. She worked around the camp, brought in wood, and continued her never-ending task of weaving and tarring baskets.

remarked that he never saw her complete one of them. She enjoyed the pork and hard tack as a change in her diet and she was particularly fond of sugar or anything sweet. She smacked her lips after eating sugar to indicate her appreciation of it. However, she could never quite shake off the eternal spectre of hunger that had hovered over her in her lonely days. She saved every scrap of leftover food from the camp, even the bones, which she took out from time to time and sucked upon vigorously. One day
shot a she otter offshore and it was brought to land to be skinned. The carcass was hauled away to the water but

protested angrily that this was a waste of good food. To humor her they left the animal's body nearby, but after it became too high even she did not argue the matter, when they finally threw it into the water.

(I wonder if this protestation was not more in the nature of a religious fear than a feeling of need for the flesh. Many tribesmen believed that the bones of water mammals should not be thrown into the rivers, lakes or sea because the spirits of the deceased animals would inform the live creatures that the human hunters didn't know how to treat their game. In
we found a huge windrow of sea mammal and fish bones stacked up. It would have been so easy to pitch these bones into the ocean fourteen or fifteen feet below the mouth of
, but instead the thousands of bones were kept, long after the flesh had been removed.)
, thinking that her shag skin garment was insufficient for her appearance in the pueblo of
, made a petticoat of mattress ticking.
started the skirt but she indicated by signs that she could do it better, so he threaded a needle and let her go to work. A man's cotton shirt and a black necktie completed her ensemble.
gave her an old cloak or cape, badly worn, but by dint of much hard work she made this into a serviceable covering.
ran into a severe storm and when the gale increased

intimated by signs that she had the power to quiet the wind. She knelt and prayed, facing into the wind and continued her chanting until at last the storm passed. Then she smiled and gave them to understand that it was only through her medicine that this had come about.
until the storm was over, then it proceeded to

became quite excited. She clapped her hands and danced gleefully on the deck. An ox-cart, plodding along the shore, was a strange and novel sight to her, and she exclaimed delightedly and pointed the wonderful phenomenon out to
. When they landed she went immediately to the ox-cart, talking all the while as she examined every part of it.
, an English physician, rode down to the beach on horseback, and this new marvel caused another outburst of excitement from
by placing her two fingers of her right hand astride the index finger of her left and imitating the motion of the horse.
was the news sensation of the day. Word of her presence spread up and down
, and received the townspeople by the score. And for several weeks she was the center of attraction, along with her shag-skin garments, her baskets, needles, etc.

delighted in visiting the pueblo, and would wander from house to house, singing and dancing when requested. She seemed particularly fond of the children, thinking perhaps of her own lost youngsters. Many of the gifts she received were given to visiting children.
understand that at one time she had
was probably a couple of years old. Said
, when queried about the fate of
which she had left asleep at the time:
I understand she made signs so plain—she showed two fingers for
and she shook her breast; one was sucking and the other had teeth. She sucked her fingers
Although various Indians were brought to see and talk with
had learned through sign language that
believed the dogs had killed and eaten it. There were many dogs running wild on
at that time. By signs also she made her questioners understand that at the time of embarkation,
had probably been taken on board the
sailed away leaving her on the beach they had shouted "manana" at her, indicating they would return the next day . . . but they never came back.
said that the dress brought ashore from Juana María's hut was given by
, who in turn sent it to

"She had bone needles with eyes drilled in and she had a knife made with bone, and another with a piece of wood that she had when I catched her cleaning the sealskin. She had nails pointed for catching fish with. She had the rope nicely twisted with sinews and had it twisted as true as any ropemaker could make, and she had bottles made of grass about the size of a gallon demijohn. We found some dishes of wood with handles."
Juana María's life ashore was all too short.
Although she entered into the activities of her new world with a child-like zest, civilization was too much for her.
Her new clothes were her prized possessions. Everyone was her friend. In vain did
attempt to piece together the history of her island life. Indians from
visited her and tried to understand her dialect, but with no results. She acquired a few words of Spanish. In her own tongue (presumably) man was nache, the sky tegua, and a hide or pelt was toco.
tata or sometimes nana. (These are colloquial terms of endearment meaning "daddy" and "grandma" respectively.[)]
She was very fond of the new foodstuffs given her at

[Nidever's]—especially green corn, vegetables and fresh fruits. When first brought ashore she was in excellent health, but her excessive use of the strange foods brought on attacks of dysentery. One day she fell from the Nidever porch, injuring her spine and probably causing internal injuries as well.
nursed her as best she could, but

grew weaker and weaker[.] Thinking to tempt her failing appetite, a seal was killed and roasted and some of the meat brought to her. Even this did not rouse her. She laughed feebly and rubbed her fingers across her badly worn teeth, making signs that now she was too old, and her teeth too dull, to eat seal. She lingered four weeks, then died. She had only been in
seven weeks from the time she had been brought ashore from
to the day of her death.
to take her on board and exhibit her up and down
as a side-show curiosity. When asked why he did not do it,
answered that he thought it didn't look right to sell a person. It may be that
, was the man who made the offer. After Juana María's death, which occurred Tuesday, October 18, 1853,
a pitched water bottle, a stone mortar and some stone beads which had been among the possessions of
deposited these items in the collections of the

What happenec [sic] to the other shag-skin garment?
My belief is that it too, unless by some miracle saved from the same fate as the water bottle, went up in the holocaust of April 1906.
The Daily Alta California, Sunday[,] November 13, 1853, carried a relatively commonplace item about a Ladies' Fair which was to be held in the
Musical Hall on Bush Street, San Francisco
on the evening of Tuesday the 15th of November.

A raffle was to be held and "a great variety of curiosities will be on exhibition, some of which, from their rarity and peculiarity, will be well worth the attention of the scientific and the curious.
